content.text: Fast, Modern DataFrame Processing with Polars

Python's data ecosystem has long been dominated by pandas — but pandas was designed in an era before multi-core CPUs were standard, before datasets routinely exceeded RAM, and before columnar data formats became the norm. Polars is a next-generation DataFrame library written in Rust that fixes every major structural weakness pandas has, delivering dramatically faster performance with lower memory usage and a cleaner API — all while staying fully within the Python data ecosystem.
Polars is an open-source DataFrame library for Python (and natively Rust) built from the ground up for
speed, memory efficiency, and large-scale data processing
. It is written entirely in Rust and uses the
as its in-memory representation. This is not a wrapper around pandas or any C extension — it is an entirely independent engine.
Polars supports two modes of execution:
— expressions are evaluated immediately, like pandas. Useful for interactive exploration.
— expressions are collected into a query plan and optimized before execution. This is the primary mode for production pipelines.
The library was created by Ritchie Vink and first released in 2020. As of 2024–2025, it has become the go-to replacement for pandas in performance-critical Python data pipelines.
Pandas has several deep architectural problems that cannot be fixed without a complete rewrite:
— pandas uses one CPU core for most operations.
Row-based memory layout for many operations
— pandas often requires 5–10× the dataset size in RAM during transformations.
— every operation is executed exactly as written, even if a more efficient plan exists.
Slow string and nested data handling
— Python object dtype is notoriously inefficient.
Polars eliminates all of these by being
, and expressing all data operations through a
For data pipelines processing tens of millions to hundreds of millions of rows, the difference is not marginal — Polars is typically
because Polars streams data in chunks.
Polars uses all available CPU cores automatically. Operations like

run in parallel without any configuration. On a modern 8-core laptop, this alone gives an 8× theoretical speedup over single-threaded pandas, and in practice the gain is often larger due to better algorithmic choices.

The Arrow columnar format stores data in tight, cache-friendly arrays. Operations return new Arrow buffers without copying data unnecessarily. Polars' lazy mode additionally allows the query planner to
(stop reading after enough rows are found) — all of which reduce memory usage dramatically.
Every column has a strict, known dtype. There is no
dtype escape hatch. This eliminates entire classes of bugs that are common in pandas pipelines.
The expression system is uniform — the same
syntax works in select, with_columns, filter, and groupby contexts.
The lazy engine supports streaming execution, processing data in chunks that never fully load into RAM. This makes it possible to process files larger than available memory.
Polars DataFrames can be zero-copy converted to and from PyArrow Tables, NumPy arrays, and pandas DataFrames. It reads and writes Parquet, CSV, JSON, Avro, IPC, and Delta Lake.
The expression API is different from pandas. Developers accustomed to
loops must learn a new paradigm. The transition takes real effort.

Many libraries — scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, statsmodels — expect pandas DataFrames or NumPy arrays. Polars does not yet have the same breadth of third-party integrations, though interoperability bridges exist.

Polars is a single-machine library. It uses all cores on one machine but cannot distribute work across a cluster like Apache Spark or Dask can. For datasets that genuinely require multiple machines, Polars is not a replacement for distributed systems.
When using lazy mode, errors are not raised until
is called. Stack traces can be less intuitive than eager pandas errors.
Polars DataFrames are largely immutable. In-place mutation is not the standard pattern, which requires rethinking some imperative pandas workflows.
Polars stores data in Apache Arrow buffers. In a columnar layout, all values of a single column are stored contiguously in memory. When computing

, the CPU reads one contiguous block of float64 values — maximally cache-efficient. Pandas stores row data in contiguous blocks per dtype, which is less efficient for column-at-a-time analytical queries.
Arrow also defines a standard binary format for inter-process data sharing. This means Polars DataFrames can be passed to PyArrow, DuckDB, or other Arrow-native tools

library for data parallelism. When computing a groupby aggregation, the data is partitioned across threads, each thread computes local aggregations, and results are merged. This happens automatically for virtually all operations — there is no
parameter to set, no multiprocessing Pool to manage.
is called on a DataFrame (or when reading from files with
— a tree of operations representing what you want, not yet how to compute it.
is called, the engine runs several optimization passes:
operations are moved as early in the plan as possible, so fewer rows are processed downstream.
— columns not needed in the final result are dropped as early as possible, reducing I/O and memory.
style operations are propagated to the scan layer, so only
— if the same computation appears twice, it is computed once and cached.
— smaller tables are used on the build side of hash joins.
This optimization step is what makes lazy mode substantially faster than eager mode for complex pipelines, even ignoring streaming.
The Polars expression API is its most distinctive feature. Expressions are
composable, lazy descriptions of column transformations

describes: for each product group, sum the revenue of APAC rows. This kind of window-filtered aggregation would require awkward multi-step pandas code. In Polars it is a single expression, and it can appear inside
not evaluated until the DataFrame context executes them
, so the engine can parallelize multiple expressions in the same

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Python API Layer                   │
│  pl.scan_parquet() / pl.read_csv() / DataFrame  │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                     │  Logical Plan (LazyFrame)
┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐
│           Logical Query Planner                 │
│  - Predicate Push-Down                          │
│  - Projection Push-Down                         │
│  - Slice Push-Down                              │
│  - CSE (Common Subexpression Elimination)       │
│  - Join Reordering                              │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                     │  Optimized Physical Plan
┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐
│         Physical Execution Engine (Rust)        │
│  - Multi-threaded via Rayon                     │
│  - SIMD-accelerated arithmetic                  │
│  - Streaming executor (chunked for large data)  │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                     │
┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐
│           Apache Arrow Memory Buffers           │
│  (columnar, cache-friendly, zero-copy sharing)  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
), the physical executor processes data in chunks of a configurable size rather than loading the entire dataset, enabling out-of-core execution.
Processing datasets from a few million to a few hundred million rows on a single machine.
Building ETL/ELT pipelines that transform raw data into analytical outputs.
Reading and processing large Parquet files where only a subset of columns or rows is needed.
Speed and memory usage are constraints — Polars will be faster than pandas in virtually every scenario.
Replacing pandas in any pipeline that does not depend heavily on scikit-learn or visualization libraries.
Building data validation, feature engineering, or aggregation layers for ML pipelines.
The dataset genuinely requires multiple machines (use Spark, Dask, or Ray).
Deep integration with the pandas-specific ecosystem is required (e.g., heavy use of pandas-native statsmodels APIs).
The team is entirely unfamiliar with it and the timeline does not allow for the learning curve.

pip install polars

# For full ecosystem (cloud storage, Excel, databases, etc.)
pip install 'polars[all]'

# For specific extras
pip install 'polars[pyarrow,connectorx,xlsx2csv,fsspec]'

import polars as pl

# Create a DataFrame
df = pl.DataFrame({
    "product": ["A", "B", "A", "C", "B", "A"],
    "region":  ["US", "US", "EU", "EU", "US", "APAC"],
    "revenue": [100, 200, 150, 300, 250, 180],
    "units":   [10,  20,  15,  30,  25,  18],
})

# Select specific columns with expressions
result = df.select([
    pl.col("product"),
    pl.col("revenue"),
    (pl.col("revenue") / pl.col("units")).alias("avg_price"),
])

print(result)

# Filter then group by and aggregate
summary = (
    df
    .filter(pl.col("region").is_in(["US", "EU"]))
    .group_by("product")
    .agg([
        pl.col("revenue").sum().alias("total_revenue"),
        pl.col("units").mean().alias("avg_units"),
        pl.col("region").n_unique().alias("region_count"),
    ])
    .sort("total_revenue", descending=True)
)

# Reading from disk — always prefer scan_ functions in lazy mode
result = (
    pl.scan_parquet("data/sales_*.parquet")   # glob pattern supported
    .filter(pl.col("date") >= "2024-01-01")   # predicate pushed to scan layer
    .select(["product", "region", "revenue"]) # only these columns are read
    .group_by(["product", "region"])
    .agg(pl.col("revenue").sum())
    .sort("revenue", descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .collect()  # Execute the full optimized plan
)
with a filter and select causes Polars to read only the needed columns and only the row groups where the filter condition might be true — this is called
and can reduce I/O by 90%+ on large files.

# Process a file larger than available RAM
result = (
    pl.scan_parquet("data/huge_file_50gb.parquet")
    .filter(pl.col("status") == "active")
    .group_by("customer_id")
    .agg(pl.col("amount").sum())
    .collect(streaming=True)  # process in chunks, never fully in RAM
)

# Compute running totals and group stats alongside raw rows
df_with_stats = df.with_columns([
    pl.col("revenue").sum().over("product").alias("product_total"),
    pl.col("revenue").rank(descending=True).over("region").alias("rank_in_region"),
    pl.col("revenue").cum_sum().over("product").alias("cumulative_revenue"),
])

import polars as pl

df = pl.scan_csv("events.csv").with_columns([
    pl.col("timestamp").str.to_datetime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"),
    pl.col("user_id").str.strip_chars().str.to_uppercase(),
    pl.col("event_type").str.contains("click").alias("is_click"),
])

orders = pl.scan_parquet("orders.parquet")
customers = pl.scan_parquet("customers.parquet")

joined = orders.join(
    customers,
    left_on="customer_id",
    right_on="id",
    how="left",
).select([
    "order_id", "amount", "customer_name", "country"
]).collect()

# Polars → pandas (zero-copy where possible)
pandas_df = polars_df.to_pandas()

# pandas → Polars
polars_df = pl.from_pandas(pandas_df)

# Polars → PyArrow Table (zero-copy)
arrow_table = polars_df.to_arrow()

# PyArrow → Polars
polars_df = pl.from_arrow(arrow_table)

# Using connectorx for fast multi-threaded SQL reading
df = pl.read_database_uri(
    query="SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01'",
    uri="postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db",
)
Example 1 — ETL Pipeline with Multiple Transformations

import polars as pl
from pathlib import Path

def build_daily_revenue_report(input_dir: Path, output_path: Path) -> None:
    result = (
        pl.scan_parquet(input_dir / "transactions_*.parquet")
        .filter(
            (pl.col("status") == "completed") &
            (pl.col("amount") > 0)
        )
        .with_columns([
            pl.col("created_at").dt.date().alias("date"),
            pl.col("amount").cast(pl.Float64),
        ])
        .group_by(["date", "product_category", "country"])
        .agg([
            pl.col("amount").sum().alias("gross_revenue"),
            pl.col("amount").mean().alias("avg_order_value"),
            pl.col("order_id").n_unique().alias("order_count"),
        ])
        .with_columns([
            (pl.col("gross_revenue") / pl.col("order_count"))
            .alias("revenue_per_order")
        ])
        .sort(["date", "gross_revenue"], descending=[False, True])
        .collect(streaming=True)
    )
    
    result.write_parquet(output_path, compression="zstd")
    print(f"Written {len(result):,} rows to {output_path}")

def build_user_features(events: pl.LazyFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    return (
        events
        .filter(pl.col("event_date") >= "2024-01-01")
        .group_by("user_id")
        .agg([
            pl.col("session_id").n_unique().alias("total_sessions"),
            pl.col("page_views").sum().alias("total_page_views"),
            pl.col("purchase_amount").sum().alias("total_spend"),
            pl.col("purchase_amount").filter(
                pl.col("purchase_amount") > 0
            ).count().alias("purchase_count"),
            pl.col("event_date").max().alias("last_active_date"),
            pl.col("event_date").min().alias("first_active_date"),
        ])
        .with_columns([
            (pl.col("total_spend") / pl.col("purchase_count").clip(lower_bound=1))
            .alias("avg_order_value"),
            (pl.col("last_active_date") - pl.col("first_active_date"))
            .dt.total_days()
            .alias("days_active_span"),
        ])
        .collect()
    )

A SaaS company processes clickstream event logs from their web product. Each day produces approximately 800 million events (~120 GB of raw JSON), which are first converted to Parquet during ingestion. A daily pipeline must:
Read 7 days of Parquet files (roughly 840 GB total).
Filter to verified user events only.
Compute per-user engagement metrics (sessions, page views, feature usage).
Join with the CRM table (~5 million customers) to append account tier.
Write the output feature table to Parquet for the ML training pipeline.
With pandas, this pipeline runs out of memory on a 256 GB machine and requires chunking logic that adds hundreds of lines of code. With Polars:

import polars as pl

(
    pl.scan_parquet("s3://data/events/date=2024-*/")   # 7-day glob
    .filter(
        (pl.col("user_verified") == True) &
        (pl.col("event_type").is_in(["page_view", "click", "purchase"]))
    )
    .group_by("user_id")
    .agg([
        pl.col("session_id").n_unique().alias("sessions_7d"),
        pl.col("event_type").filter(pl.col("event_type") == "purchase")
          .count().alias("purchases_7d"),
        pl.col("revenue").sum().alias("revenue_7d"),
    ])
    .join(
        pl.scan_parquet("s3://data/crm/customers.parquet")
          .select(["user_id", "account_tier"]),
        on="user_id",
        how="left",
    )
    .collect(streaming=True)
    .write_parquet("s3://data/features/engagement_features.parquet")
)

This runs on a single 32-core, 64 GB machine in roughly 12–18 minutes using streaming execution, consuming a peak of about 40 GB RAM. The query optimizer push-down reduces the actual bytes read from S3 to roughly 30–40% of the total file size because only the needed columns and relevant row groups are fetched.
This is the most costly mistake.
calls a Python function row-by-row, abandoning all of Rust's speed advantages. Almost every
use case in pandas has a vectorized Polars expression equivalent. Always search the Polars API first.

# WRONG — Python function called per row, ~100× slower
df.with_columns(
    pl.col("name").map_elements(lambda x: x.upper())
)

# CORRECT — vectorized Rust string operation
df.with_columns(
    pl.col("name").str.to_uppercase()
)
Converting to pandas and back destroys performance gains. When processing large data, keep everything in Polars until the final output.
builds a lazy plan and can apply projection and predicate push-down at scan time. Always use
and treating the result as if data is present. A
is a window function that preserves all rows.
reduces rows. They are different operations used in different contexts.
For datasets that exceed available RAM,
without streaming will either OOM or thrash.
Always use lazy mode for pipelines.
. This unlocks query optimization and streaming.
natively and will parallelize reads across files.
drops all columns not mentioned, which is cleaner and reduces memory if you don't need the originals.
Build long expression chains rather than materializing intermediate results:

# BETTER — single pass, optimizer sees the whole plan
result = (
    df.lazy()
    .filter(pl.col("active"))
    .with_columns([...])
    .group_by("id")
    .agg([...])
    .collect()
)
streams results directly to disk without materializing the entire output in RAM:

(
    pl.scan_parquet("huge_input.parquet")
    .filter(...)
    .group_by(...)
    .agg(...)
    .sink_parquet("output.parquet")   # streams output, does not collect first
)
Don't let schema ambiguity propagate through a pipeline. Cast columns to their correct types immediately after reading.
Polars infers CSV schemas from the first
, which can be wrong. Specify schema explicitly for production:

In independent benchmarks (TPC-H, H2O.ai), Polars consistently outperforms pandas by 10–50× on groupby and join operations, and 5–15× on filter and aggregation. It is competitive with DuckDB (which is also extremely fast) and significantly faster than Dask for single-machine workloads.
— multiple Parquet files are read by different threads simultaneously.
— within a single Parquet file, row groups are processed in parallel.
— groupby hash table construction and join builds are partitioned across threads.
There is nothing to configure — thread count defaults to the number of logical CPUs, controllable with:

— The two are complementary. DuckDB excels at complex SQL queries with optimizer maturity; Polars excels at Python-native expression pipelines. Both speak Arrow natively, so they interoperate with zero copy. Many pipelines use DuckDB for SQL ingestion and Polars for downstream transformation.
— Natural pairing. Parquet I/O, Arrow IPC, and Arrow Flight all work natively through PyArrow. Polars uses PyArrow's Parquet reader under the hood on some platforms.
— Ibis is a portable DataFrame API with a Polars backend, enabling backend-agnostic data code.
enables reading and writing Delta tables (the transactional table format used in Databricks and many modern lakehouses) with Polars.
— Because Polars DataFrames serialize to Arrow efficiently, they integrate well with Arrow Flight servers or REST APIs that return columnar data.
— Polars pipelines work seamlessly as tasks inside orchestration frameworks. The lazy/collect pattern maps cleanly onto task boundaries.
— abstraction layer that writes code compatible with both pandas and Polars
— both accept Polars DataFrames for visualization
— ultra-fast database reader that outputs Arrow, consumed directly by Polars

— DuckDB is an in-process SQL engine that is similarly fast and also uses Arrow internally. DuckDB is better when the query logic is naturally SQL and when running ad-hoc analytics. Polars is better when building Python-native programmatic pipelines with complex, conditional logic that is awkward in SQL.
Polars raises errors at collection time if types are violated. Unlike pandas, there is no silent coercion to
dtype that masks type errors. For production pipelines, define schema explicitly at read time to catch data quality issues early.
, not at expression definition time. Wrap
in try/except and include plan introspection in error logging:
When reading from untrusted sources (S3, user-uploaded files), validate file format and schema before running the full pipeline. Use

Rust's ownership model eliminates entire classes of memory bugs — buffer overflows, use-after-free, and data races are impossible in safe Rust. This makes Polars significantly more reliable than C-extension-based alternatives under concurrent access.
Polars uses Arrow's validity bitmask to represent nulls, separate from the data buffer. This means null-handling is explicit and consistent — there is no
ambiguity as in pandas. Explicitly check for nulls with
Lazy query plans are deterministic. Given the same input and same plan, Polars always produces the same output. There are no hidden global states or random seeds affecting standard operations.
Think of a Polars lazy pipeline the same way you think of a SQL query plan. When you write
in SQL, the database does not execute each clause in order — it builds a plan and optimizes it before touching data. Polars lazy mode is exactly this, expressed in Python.
The expression API is analogous to SQL column expressions:
. Once this mental mapping is internalized, the entire Polars expression vocabulary becomes intuitive.
Polars is a Rust-native DataFrame library that uses Apache Arrow columnar memory and automatic multi-threading, making it 5–50× faster than pandas for typical analytical workloads.
The lazy execution engine applies query optimizations (predicate push-down, projection push-down, CSE) automatically, reducing both compute time and memory usage.
) enables processing datasets larger than RAM on a single machine.
The expression API is the core abstraction — vectorized, composable expressions replace all
Polars is a single-machine library and should be combined with Spark, Dask, or Ray when the workload genuinely requires horizontal scaling across multiple nodes.
) in production pipelines to enable lazy optimization and Parquet predicate push-down.
Polars is now the default choice for production Python data pipelines on single machines, with pandas reserved for small-scale exploration and legacy ecosystem integrations.
